author: @Hukkinen
text:
I'm missing easy way to reuse things in FreeCAD. I've been using FreeCAD for half a year now. 

I should kook into SubShapeBinder. However, I've read you can only replicate the tip of a PartDesign Body (feature stack).

But I'd like to replicate from below the tip! Because after designing one Body, I often need a variation of it and I realize this at this point! This is my work flow and FreeCAD should accommodate my user experience. 

At this point I could dismatle and move the feature stack, but moving often breaks things or is not straightforward at all. All Attachments (positiining to Local Coordinate System) should be removed AND disabled, to allow PartDesign "Move to different body" to work . And this is tedious, because I have to rethink the Attachments; Attachments are half the cognitive work I have to do in my head when designing and building complex parts.

So, I mostly copy-paste or carbon copy Sketches now.

I'm using Assembly4 now. It makes PartDesign coherent.

author: @JasonSheehan-fy3lb
text: There are lots of great freecad y/t channels.. but for the absolute best by far for the cam workbench is ; open source cnc.. he’s covering every aspect of cam with some great hacks .. also for some incredible 3D surfacing; deltahedra , check out his bicycle saddle vid .. again welcome to the freecad universe  !! I’m a luthier with 3 cnc’s and a few 3D printers.. I’ve designed and built everything on freecad form guitars to luthiery tools/jigs to vintage motorcycle parts and beyond.. freecad has its flaws but the last 2-3yrs have seen some incredible advancements in its ability.. please encourage others to keep supporting the devs !! Cheers !

author: @DuncsWorkshop
text: Brilliant, I have been trying to get to grips with this infuriatingly excellent yet non intuitive free program for the last week and making progress, but slow progress. Your tutorial is certainly helping, Thank you.  The one major procedure I have found after opening a new file is that you should always start with 'Create Part' then 'Create Body' and then usually, but not always, 'Create Sketch'.  One request please, would it be possible to change your mouse pointer to extra large?  It might make it easier to see which buttons you're clicking.  Many Thanks Duncan

author: @bascomnextion5639
text: The spread sheet parametric design I found very useful for making cases for projects with it you make your part/object say a box with a lid but you reference the constraints to the spread sheet and you only need to change the value in the spread sheet to adjust the size, Adventures in creation has FreeCAD Electronic Enclosure Using Simple Parametric Design Techniques this tutorial is a step by step process and is a good way for a newcomer to learn (it was for me). Also they call it a pocket tool as you can make blind holes pockets with it or even a void inside a solid object. Also companies in china now offer 3D manufacturing at very low prices so even if you do not have the 3D printer or milling machine you can have it made they could make Roberts metal parts for him.

author: @tenij000
text:
i use this to real cool  whit the conolse can use this for example to make part import FreeCAD
import Part
import Mesh
import Import

def maak_en_exporteer_meerdere_objecten(basis_lijst, gaten, export_pad="output.stl"):
    doc = FreeCAD.newDocument()
    totale_vorm = None

    # 1. Basisobjecten maken en samenvoegen
    for config in basis_lijst:
        shape_type = config.get("type", "box")
        x = config.get("x_pos", 0.0)
        y = config.get("y_pos", 0.0)
        z = config.get("z_pos", 0.0)

        if shape_type == "box":
            l = config.get("length", 10.0)
            w = config.get("width", 10.0)
            h = config.get("height", 10.0)
            vorm = Part.makeBox(l, w, h)
            vorm.Placement = FreeCAD.Placement(FreeCAD.Vector(x, y, z), FreeCAD.Rotation())
        elif shape_type == "cylinder":
            r = config.get("radius", 5.0)
            h = config.get("height", 10.0)
            vorm = Part.makeCylinder(r, h)
            vorm.Placement = FreeCAD.Placement(FreeCAD.Vector(x, y, z), FreeCAD.Rotation())
        else:
            print(f"Onbekend type: {shape_type}")
            continue

        if totale_vorm is None:
            totale_vorm = vorm
        else:
            totale_vorm = totale_vorm.fuse(vorm)

    # 2. Gaten erin maken
    for hole in gaten:
        hole_type = hole.get("hole_type", "round")
        x = hole.get("x_pos", 0.0)
        y = hole.get("y_pos", 0.0)
        z = hole.get("z_pos", 0.0)
        d = hole.get("depth", 10.0)

        if hole_type == "round":
            r = hole.get("radius", 5.0)
            gat = Part.makeCylinder(r, d)
            gat.Placement = FreeCAD.Placement(FreeCAD.Vector(x, y, z), FreeCAD.Rotation())
        elif hole_type == "square":
            l = hole.get("length", 10.0)
            w = hole.get("width", 10.0)
            gat = Part.makeBox(l, w, d)
            gat.Placement = FreeCAD.Placement(FreeCAD.Vector(x - l/2, y - w/2, z), FreeCAD.Rotation())
        else:
            print(f"Onbekend hole_type: {hole_type}")
            continue

        totale_vorm = totale_vorm.cut(gat)

    # 3. Toevoegen aan document
    part_obj = doc.addObject("Part::Feature", "GecombineerdObject")
    part_obj.Shape = totale_vorm
# Gebruik exportpad dat door de knop wordt meegegeven, anders standaard


    # 4. Exporteer STL
    Mesh.export([part_obj], export_pad)
    print(f"STL geëxporteerd naar {export_pad}")
    # Gebruik exportpad dat door de knop wordt meegegeven, anders standaard

    # 5. Exporteer STEP
    # Controleer of export_pad een absoluut pad is, anders sla op in C:\freecad\
    if not os.path.isabs(export_pad):
        export_pad = os.path.join("C:\\freecad", export_pad)

    # Zorg dat exportmap bestaat
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(export_pad), exist_ok=True)

    step_pad = export_pad.replace(".stl", ".step")
    Import.export([part_obj], step_pad)
    print(f"STEP geëxporteerd naar {step_pad}")

# === Voorbeeldgebruik ===
if __name__ == "__main__":
    basis_config = [
        {"type": "box", "length": 100, "width": 50, "height": 10, "x_pos": 0, "y_pos": 0, "z_pos": 0},
        {"type": "box", "length": 25, "width": 25, "height": 25, "x_pos": 0, "y_pos": 0, "z_pos": 10},
        {"type": "cylinder", "radius": 10, "height": 20, "x_pos": 110, "y_pos": 10, "z_pos": 0},
        {"type": "cylinder", "radius": 5, "height": 15, "x_pos": -20, "y_pos": 40, "z_pos": 0}
    ]

    gaten_config = [
        {"type": "hole", "hole_type": "round", "radius": 5, "depth": 20, "x_pos": 20, "y_pos": 25, "z_pos": 0},
        {"type": "hole", "hole_type": "square", "length": 10, "width": 10, "depth": 20, "x_pos": 60, "y_pos": 25, "z_pos": 0},
        {"type": "hole", "hole_type": "round", "radius": 5, "depth": 20, "x_pos": 110, "y_pos": 10, "z_pos": 0}
    ]

    maak_en_exporteer_meerdere_objecten(basis_config, gaten_config, "object_met_gaten.stl")

author: @GabrielPettier
text:
25:01 I'm trying to follow along, but you jumped a bit fast there, about how you build the 4 intermediate circles that have a 45° offset from the first 4 that lay on axis, I think you used computed values and horizontal+vertical constraints, but wasn't really happy with that, I tried using a distance from center constraint at 30mm, and i think an angled constraint would do the trick, but then I need a line, and to attach it to the center of the circle, and the center of the plan, seems to work but i had to use blocking constraints to hold my lines in places after manually setting one of their end to 0,0, only the circle end seemed to attach automatically (and then hide my lines). But, after that, my shape couldn't be extruded, because of these pesky lines, so i had to delete them, thankfully the circles remained in place, and i put block constraints directly on them, and the rest worked fine.

Anyway, a long time happened between starting to type that comment and finding the solution, hope it helps someone 😆.

text: I completely failed to add the circles on the 45 degree angles. replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: use the line tool to create a line 30mm long - dimension it to the axis and you will get a degree setting - set that at 45 the draw your circle and make it co-incident with the far end of the line

replies.author: @rustyshackleford8043
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thanks Robert, that's helped, Though my line is now over-constrained apparently.

A tip for anyone else struggling - right click on the thing you are trying to constrain in the elements box and select 'Select Constraints' it will now only show the constraints for the thing you are interested in.

author: @steini19o4
text:
It´s nice seeing you finally picking up parametric CAD software. Try thinking about which parameters you might want to adjust in a sketch and then constrain the sketch entirely relative to these parameters.

Using construction geometry (the blue one) in the sketch workbench opens up the whole greek geometric math (and beyond) to freely position other geometry around. You can also import geometric features from the existing part into the sketch workbencch (e.g. points, lines, circles), which are then projected onto the working plane (so you can even import geometry not within the current plane). This allows you to accurately dimension entire sketches without entering a single number and entire parts with only a single parameter.

I then recommend experimenting with symmetry and arrays. After that look into linear and polar patterns in the part design workbench. And if you want to specify a lot of parameters in one place, try the spreadsheet workbenches, almost everything that takes a number in freecad can also accept a spreadsheet reference.

With these tools designing most parts becomes very efficient and intuitive.

Also no need to manually switch between the part design and sketch workbenches. Just click on the surface of the part or plane you want to sketch on and create a new sketch, you will be automatically moved between workbenches.

author: @sleepib
text: I usually use the gear workbench for the tooth geometry. Really nice for things like bevel gears.  For hole patterns in general i usually draw one circle and  use polar pattern on the pocket/hole operation.  If I want an 8 hole pattern in a sketch I might use the octagon tool to make construction geometry to attach the circles to. That way if I want to change the size of the hole pattern later I only have to change one dimension rather than one or two for each circle. Similarly for the hole diameters, I'd use the equal constraint instead of having them all dimensioned 20mm.

author: @wimwiddershins
text: I use Solidworks at work and Freecad 1.0 at home. Both are great. Freecad has an involute Gear workbench plugin that is very easy to use. Amazing for free! Freecad is under enthusiastic development and there have been some significant usability improvements in the last 2 years

author: @prototype9000
text: freecad allways seg faults on me as soon as i use any menus

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ok - don't know what a seg fault is

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text: I got a couple crashes as well with the AppImage on Linux, but i managed to follow along the video, not sure what triggered them but we might have bugs to report…

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Segmentation Fault is the term for when a program tries to do something to memory the OS says it shouldn't (usually because it's memory allocated to another program). If you ever had a Blue Screen Of Death, that was one possible consequences, thankfully these days it only crashes the program because the OS knows how not to panic about it and just terminate the program, but it's usually sad because you lose some work anyway. Thankfully (again) FreeCAD has autosaves and allow recovery on restart.

replies.author: @prototype9000
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering on linux its the equivent of a crash with a dump of machine readable code usually in the form opcodes in the terminal or debug log

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Oh - I think I used to call that a stack overflow! But it has been a while since I coded operating systems lol

author: @johnbarr9857

author: @tdtrecordsmusic
text:
i use the realthunder version ...    i like being able to modify designs after the fact.   Normal/default FC has a funk > when changes you make result in new faces or the removal of faces > it will go blah poo huh rmmmph , & melt in errors. =topological naming bug.  Sometime soon, the realthunder fixes will be included in normal freecad  ...    IF YOU continue with FC normal and this error happens, you just have to delete and remodel. Save often. . & don't fret if it goes kapooph. its not YOU. its the program.

     Other than that >> FreeCAD is hands down the most important tool in the maker world. Open source. Community driven. its the precipice of our culture. The most ultimate invention. the invention that makes inventions !!!!  A shining achievement of humanity. >> that normal people can create the same stuff that corporations demand our slavery for.             

    Once you get assemblies worked out + can FEM an idea and verify that in reality with IRL parts = you know u have arrived : ) 

Definite first steps are to learn > top down, bottom up & middle out design strategies. 

p.s  Don't sleep on learning surfaces/surface modeling + 3d sketching ... but that comes after the basics.   A YT channel called Mangojelly has a bunch of good tutorials & is my go to for learning new tips/tricks. awesome stuff

replies.author: @tdtrecordsmusic
replies.text: oh yeah & definitely learn using reference dimensions / spreadsheet   ... lotta newcomers learning via DIY miss that VITAL paradigm . it is a VITAL / fundamental technique

Welcome to FreeCad  glade you finally got round to leaning about it, have be using it for a good few years now. What i loved about it from the start was the software is free and you keep all your files on your own computer not on cloud. No subscription

author: @velcroman11
text: This is a demonstration I call “onion learning”.  Show a student “the onion”. Ie the overall appearance of the thing, the over view.  We can see what the thing is, an onion.  Now we peel off the surface covering and see the next level that is used to make the “onion”, the thing.  We keep doing this with each ring of the onion thing learning more about why the onion thing is made to look the way it looks.  There is a more “modern”  name,  “reverse engineering”.  It’s a good name and does explain what it does but I prefer the term “onion learning but many people think that name stinks.  😅

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a very good description mate lol

author: @adamcraig1012

author: @theosib
text: FreeCAD isn't like commercial CAD programs with consistent UIs and a unified design team and dedicated testing team. It's developed like a lot of other open source software, where random contributors add little bits over time based on what they feel like adding. This means that how you solve problems in FreeCAD is not always straightforward and often involves a lot of tribal knowledge you have to get from forums and Discord. This makes FreeCAD less of a CAD program and more of a life commitment. 😅

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nah mate - you can see it like that if you want - but my brother works in commercial software development for a company and according to him that is far more chaotic and fractured it just looks pretty. Truth is all CAD programs have to perform CAD functions and they all do - what you choose is just a choice that suits you then you find reasons to defend your choice. I am not saying that's a good or bad thing - it just is lol. The other thing is - It would not have mattered one jot what I chose - someone was bound to say it was a bad choice and give a whole bunch of reasons why. If you think other software is better fair enough - do some tutorials on that. But to write a comment on here - that adds nothing does it? - it just makes you feel good about yourself.

replies.author: @theosib
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringDon’t get me wrong. I use exclusively FreeCAD and OpenSCAD. I’m just aware that they don’t have the polish and ease of learning of commercial options.  The advantage of FreeCAD is that the community is very helpful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ahh right - i got hold of the wrong end of the stick - I am sorry most mate for my lack of understanding and i hope you didn't find my comment rude - There is a strong element of that in FreeCAD that's for sure

replies.author: @ivolol
replies.text: They've had a lot of help from Ondsel contributing to them in a bit more structured and focused manner the past few years though, which I'd say is what enabled them to get 'over the line' to a 1.0 release

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: This will be an excellent video series for those wanting to learn FreeCAD. I can't wait to see which holes you fill in for me. Remember by starting with a sketch rather then primitives, you can change the sketch and not need to start the process over. Primitives are a one and done. Sketches are adjustable.

FreeCAD is completely parametric, so you can change any parameters of the additive shape (Primitive) at any point. B

But I agree that sketches have a lot more utility.

author: @AlanBell
text: I have been using FreeCad for years and never used the part design workbench at all, I was always using the part workbench and working more like TinkerCad, adding primitives and doing unions and cuts and so on. I found the spreadsheet tool is quite good for putting in parameters of your design, so you can change a value there and then use it in dimensions all over the place. This video is a great introduction to the part design area, loads more for me to play with!

author: @NobleValerian
text:
Holy wow!  They actually made it to 1.0!!!  Last time I used it, it was hot garbage.  Looks like it's been upgraded to more usable room-temp recycling now.  Another 12 years and it might be half as good as the paid stuff we have now.

Only half kidding.  It's nice that the project exists and is freely available, but using it a couple years ago was just about the most miserable experience I've ever had with a program.  That experience entirely convinced me that up to $1000 on a perpetual software license was more than justified (while I was fortunate enough to have that money briefly available), and I still don't have any regrets spending that money.

(FYI, I went with Alibre and got an amazing deal on Expert, and I would definitely recommend Atom3D as a nice option when they have a nice sale, even for hobbyists.  You could just email and tell them what your budget is, too.  They're a generous company, responsive and nice to work with.)
